content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

October 7, 2016

John J. Duran, Esq.
THE DURAN LAW GROUP
A Professional Law Corporation
9000 W. Sunset Boulevard #710
West Hollywood, CA 90069

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-16-203

Dear Mr. Duran:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding campaign and "behested payment" provisions of Section 82015 in the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090.

QUESTION

As a City Council member in West Hollywood, would you have to report as a "behested payment" a $10,000 payment to Gay Men's Chorus of Los Angeles that you personally solicited?

CONCLUSION

The payment made at your behest must be reported as behested payment on Commission Form 803. The Form 803 must be filed with your city within 30 days of the date the payment was made.

FACTS

You are a part time City Council member in West Hollywood and also serve on the Board of Directors of the Gay Men's Chorus of Los Angeles, a 39-year-old 501(c)(3) organization that supports a performing arts group. You serve on the board of the Chorus to assist them with fundraising and legal issues. You serve as Chairman of the Board and run the meetings. You are also one of 250 singing members in the Chorus. You said you have been a member of the Chorus

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-16-203
Page No. 2

for 18 years, well before you became an elected official in 2001. You are not employed by the
Chorus and receive no salary or benefits.

The Chorus has started a program called the "Alive Music Project" which is an anti-bullying
program using music as the vehicle (the "Project"). You have performed for over 35,000 high
school students over the past eight years.

Recently, you were at a public event with State Senator Ben Allen. At the event, a
discussion about the proper role of art in the public high schools came up between the Senator and a
constituent and you told the Senator about the Project. When the event concluded, another attendee
approached you and said he wanted to learn more about the Chorus and its program. You gave him
your law firm business card and told him to call or email to discuss further. He subsequently
contacted you and after a discussion, he said he was interested in making a donation to support the
Project. You gave him the contact information for the Chorus and on September 27, 2016, he made
a contribution of $10,000 to the Chorus to support the Project. You did not solicit any specific
dollar amount.

ANALYSIS

Section 82015 of the Act requires elected officers to report payments made at the officer's
behest that would not otherwise be reported as contributions. The purpose of the "bechested
payment" reporting requirements is to capture reporting for payments that are not direct
contributions to elected officials, but payments in which the public would have an interest, given
the official's role in the exchange. For purposes of bechested payment reporting, a payment is made
at the behest of an elected officer if it is made under the control or at the direction of, in
cooperation, consultation, coordination, or concert with, at the request or suggestion of, or with the
express, prior consent of the officer. (Regulation 18215.3(a); Regulation 18225.7.)

A payment is considered a reportable bechested payment if it is clear from the surrounding
circumstances that the payment was made for purposes unrelated to the officer's candidacy for
elective office and is made principally for a charitable purpose, as here. A bechested payment must
be reported within 30 days following the date on which the payment or payments equal or exceed
$5,000 in the aggregate from the same source in the same calendar year in which they are made.
Once a single source has made a bechested payment of $5,000 or more during the calendar year,
subsequent payments of any amount from that source must be reported.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,
Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By _______________
John W. Wallace
Assistant General Counsel,
Legal Division

Section 82015 of the Act requires elected officers to report payments made at the officer's 
behest that would not otherwise be reported as contributions. The purpose of the "behested
